mnemonics: STRUCT is a Blueprint: Just like a blueprint defines a house's layout, a struct definition defines the data layout. It doesn't create the house (variable) itself, just the plan.
keyPoints: Use the `struct` keyword followed by a tag name (e.g., `struct Person`) to define a new composite data type.
The definition is a template; it does not allocate memory. Memory is allocated when you declare a variable of that struct type.
Members (fields) are declared inside the braces, each with its own data type (e.g., `int age; char name[50];`).
commonMistakes: Forgetting the semicolon `;` at the end of the struct definition.
Attempting to use the struct tag name without the preceding `struct` keyword (in C), e.g., using `Person p;` instead of `struct Person p;` unless a typedef has been created.
